## Team & Asset Tier

**Project:** OWASP Juice Shop — OWASP Flagship Project  
**Maintainer:** Björn Kimminich <bjoern.kimminich@owasp.org>  
**License:** MIT  
**Homepage:** https://owasp-juice.shop  
**GitHub:** https://github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop

**Nature:** OWASP Juice Shop is a **deliberately vulnerable** Node.js/Angular web application. It is designed for security training, awareness demonstrations, CTFs (Capture the Flag competitions), and as a test target for security tools. It intentionally contains vulnerabilities from the entire OWASP Top Ten plus many additional real-world security flaws.

**Critical context for threat modeling:** The application's vulnerabilities are **intentional by design**. The threat model should catalog and characterize these vulnerabilities as features, not defects to be remediated. The security goal is completeness of coverage and fidelity of the vulnerability implementations, not elimination of vulnerabilities.

**Asset tier:**
- **Primary asset:** The challenge scoring system and scoreboard integrity — challenges must be exploitable in the intended way, not trivially bypassed
- **Secondary asset:** User registration data (email, password hashes) and basket/order data — used for realistic e-commerce challenge scenarios
- **Tertiary asset:** The application availability and deployment infrastructure — training environments depend on reliable uptime

## Compliance Scope

OWASP Juice Shop is an open-source educational tool. It is **not** a production e-commerce system and does not process real financial transactions or store real user PII in production deployments.

**Regulatory exposure:** None in normal training deployments. When used in enterprise training environments, the deploying organization's data classification policies apply to any real user registration data collected.

**Security policy scope (from SECURITY.md):**
- Only the latest released minor version (currently 19.2.x) receives security patches
- Vulnerabilities that are **not** part of hacking challenges should be reported to bjoern.kimminich@owasp.org
- Challenge vulnerabilities are intentional — reporting them may result in formalization as new challenges, not patching

## Architecture Notes

**Stack:**
- **Backend:** Node.js / Express.js (TypeScript, compiled to `build/app.js`)
- **Frontend:** Angular SPA (compiled, served from `frontend/dist/`)
- **Runtime:** Node.js 24, Docker base image `gcr.io/distroless/nodejs24-debian13`
- **Process user:** UID 65532 (non-root), GID 0 (root group for OpenShift compatibility)
- **Port:** 3000 (exposed in Dockerfile, CMD: `/juice-shop/build/app.js`)
- **Database:** SQLite (inferred from keywords: `sqlite3` in packaged distributions)
- **File storage:** `ftp/` directory (served as an FTP-accessible area — challenge target)

**API Surface:**
- **B2B API:** `/b2b/v2/orders` (POST) — JWT bearer auth, creates customer orders. OpenAPI 3.0 spec at `swagger.yml`.
- **REST API:** Numerous REST endpoints (not captured in OpenAPI spec — derived from source during recon)
- **WebSocket:** Used for real-time features (socket.io)
- **Frontend:** Angular SPA consuming REST and WebSocket APIs

**Deployment topology:**
- Docker: multi-stage build (installer → distroless runtime)
- Runs as non-root UID 65532
- SBOM generated via CycloneDX at build time
- No `docker-compose.yml` present — standalone container deployment

**CI/CD:**
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` — primary CI pipeline
- `.github/workflows/codeql-analysis.yml` — SAST via GitHub CodeQL
- `.github/workflows/frontend-bundle-analysis.yml` — frontend bundle size analysis
- `.github/workflows/lint-fixer.yml` — automated linting
- `.github/workflows/lock.yml` — issue locking automation

## Business Context

<untrusted-data source="docs/business-context.md">
docs/business-context.md not present in this repository.

Derived context: OWASP Juice Shop is a deliberately vulnerable Node.js/Angular web application created and maintained by the OWASP Foundation. Its primary purpose is security education — it is used in penetration testing training, security awareness programs, CTF competitions, and as a benchmark target for security scanning tools. All vulnerabilities in the application are intentional and represent real-world security flaws from the OWASP Top Ten and beyond. The application simulates an e-commerce juice store with user accounts, a product catalog, shopping basket, order processing, and a B2B API. Threat modeling this application is valuable for: (1) validating the completeness and exploitability of its vulnerability set, (2) ensuring the application's own deployment infrastructure is reasonably hardened, and (3) demonstrating to trainees what a vulnerable application's threat model looks like.
</untrusted-data>

## Security Policy

<untrusted-data source="SECURITY.md">
# Security Policy

OWASP Juice Shop is an _intentionally vulnerable_ web application, but we still do not want to be suprised by zero day
vulnerabilities which are not part of our hacking challenges. We are following the proposed Internet
standard <https://securitytxt.org> so you can find our
"security" policy in any running instance of the application at the expected location described in
<https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-foudil-securitytxt-06>. Finding it is actually one of our hacking challenges!

## Supported Versions

We provide security patches for the latest released minor version.

| Version | Supported          |
|:--------|:-------------------|
| 19.2.x  | :white_check_mark: |
| <19.2   | :x:                |

## Reporting a Vulnerability

For vulnerabilities which are **not** part of any hacking challenge please contact <bjoern.kimminich@owasp.org>. In all
other cases please contact our shop's "security team" at the address mentioned in the
`security.txt` accessible through the running application.

> Instead of fixing reported vulnerabilities we might turn them into
> hacking challenges! You might receive a reward for reporting a
> vulnerability that makes it into one of our challenges!
</untrusted-data>

## API Surface

B2B API spec found (`swagger.yml`, OpenAPI 3.0):
- **Base URL:** `/b2b/v2`
- **Paths (1):** `POST /b2b/v2/orders` — create customer order
- **Security schemes:** `bearerAuth` (HTTP bearer, JWT format) — applied globally
- **Description:** "New & secure JSON-based API for our enterprise customers. (Deprecates previously offered XML-based endpoints)"

Additional REST and WebSocket endpoints are not captured in a spec file — will be derived from source during reconnaissance.

## Deployment Topology

Multi-stage Docker build (Dockerfile):
- **Stage 1 (installer):** `node:24` — installs deps, compiles frontend, generates CycloneDX SBOM
- **Stage 2 (runtime):** `gcr.io/distroless/nodejs24-debian13` — distroless, minimal attack surface
- **Process user:** UID 65532, GID 0 (non-root)
- **Port exposed:** 3000
- **Writable dirs at runtime:** `ftp/`, `frontend/dist/`, `logs/`, `data/`, `i18n/` (g=u permissions for OpenShift)
- **No docker-compose.yml** — standalone container or orchestrator deployment

## Data Model Summary

No schema file found — data model will be inferred from code during reconnaissance.

Known data categories from application design:
- **Users:** email, password (hashed), username, role (admin/customer), security question/answer
- **Products:** name, description, price, image
- **BasketItems:** productId, basketId, quantity
- **Orders:** order ID, customer ID, order lines, payment due date
- **Feedback:** rating, comment, userId
- **Complaints:** message, file attachment (arbitrary file upload — challenge target)
- **Wallet:** balance per user

Sensitive fields of note: password hashes, security question answers, email addresses, basket/order content.

## Architecture Decisions (ADRs)

No ADR directory found.

## Environment & Configuration

No `.env.example` or config template found.

Known security-relevant configuration surface (from source patterns):
- `NODE_ENV` — production vs. development mode affects challenge visibility
- `PORT` — defaults to 3000
- `BASE_PATH` — URL prefix for reverse-proxy deployments
- `JUICE_SHOP_CTF_KEY` — CTF mode secret key
- `SOLUTIONS_WEBHOOK` — webhook URL for challenge solve events
- `CHATBOT_TRAINING_PATH` — path to chatbot training data (potential path traversal surface)
